mnemonics.title: V-P-H Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: V-P-H = Vanishing Points lie on the Horizon line. Remember: VPH - Vision Projects to Horizon

mnemonics.title: One-Two-Three Perspective
mnemonics.content: 1-2-3 = One eye (central VP), Two walls (vertical lines stay, horizontal converge), Three axes (all three coordinate directions have their own vanishing points)

mnemonics.title: Railway Track Recall
mnemonics.content: R-T-R = Railway Tracks Recede. Parallel tracks appear to meet at the vanishing point on the horizon

mnemonics.title: COP Rememberer
mnemonics.content: COP = Center of Projection. The eye/camera location - all vanishing points are viewed from this point

acronyms.usage: The point where parallel lines converge in perspective projection. Use when calculating perspective transformations.
The horizontal line representing the viewer's eye level. All vanishing points (except vertical in 3-point) lie on this line.
The point from which the projection rays emanate (the camera or viewer's eye). Determines where vanishing points appear.
The plane onto which the 3D scene is projected. Vanishing points exist on this plane.
Classification based on number of vanishing points: 1VP = looking straight at a wall, 2VP = corner of a room, 3VP = looking up/down at a building

visualTips.visualization: Imagine standing on infinite railway tracks. The point where the two rails appear to meet on the distant horizon is your vanishing point. Draw a horizontal line through it - that's your horizon line at eye level.
Visualize standing directly in front of a long hallway or railway track. All depth lines converge to ONE point in the center. The front-facing surfaces (walls, floor) remain square/parallel.
Imagine standing at the corner of a room looking at two walls meeting. One set of lines goes left to one VP, another set goes right to another VP. Only vertical lines remain truly vertical.
Imagine looking up at a tall skyscraper from ground level. Two horizontal vanishing points on the horizon, plus a THIRD vanishing point high above (or below) for the vertical lines that now converge.
The horizon line is ALWAYS at the camera's eye level. If you raise the camera, the horizon rises. Vanishing points move up/down with the horizon line.

analogies.analogy: The point where parallel railway tracks meet on the horizon when you stand on them and look into the distance - they don't actually meet, but your eyes make them appear to.
Your eye level at the beach - where the ocean meets the sky. This is your reference line for all vanishing points in a scene.
Road lanes appearing to converge as they stretch to the mountains. The wider the road looks near you, the faster it appears to narrow in the distance.
Looking straight down a long hallway or corridor - like a hotel corridor viewed from one end.
Standing at the corner of a room looking at the two walls that meet - like looking at the corner of a cube.
Looking up at a tall building from the ground - the vertical edges seem to lean backward and meet at a point way up in the sky.
